::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In South Dakota, by statute there is a continuum of staggered parole-release formulas from the 25- to 75-percent mark based on offense severity and criminal history. For crimes committed after July 1, 2023, sentences for designated serious crimes have been made nonparolable. A large percentage of people who are incarcerated are assigned “initial parole dates” shortly after admission, with the expectation that they will be released without a hearing before the parole board if they comply with the requirements of their “individual program directives.”¹
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility In 2019, 41 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 59 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 35 percent. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}

``` ### Sex In 2019, 91 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2019, 29 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2019, 56 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 44 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drug (26 percent) offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths In 2019, 30 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state's code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who's most serious offense is non-violent, and 75 for those whose most serious offense is violent). Third, in South Dakota, the percentage of the max. sentence that corresponds to the min. sentence depends on previous sentences.Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2020, all cases were assumed to have previous sentences. Consequently, the rules-based estimated min. sentence was replaced by 50 percent of the max. sentence for those whose most serious offense was non-violent and 75 percent for those whose most serious offense was violent. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁴
## Endnotes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Bree Crye, and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: South Dakota *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, August 2023), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/south_dakota_doi_report_8_10_23.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/south_dakota_doi_report_8_10_23.pdf).

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43.

⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.“Adult Corrections 11/30/2023,” South Dakota Department of Corrections, accessed October 1, 2024, https://web.archive.org/web/20240101091645/https:/doc.sd.gov/documents/AdultPopulationNovember30203.pdf. Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 2 percent, changing from 3,434 in 2010 to 3,353 in 2022. ```{=html}
```

## Sentence Lengths In 2020, 31 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2015 to 2020, the number people released from prison increased 31 percent, dropping from 1,935 in 2015 to 2,535 in 2020. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In 2020, 33 percent of parole-eligible people released in South Dakota were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 43 percent from 2010. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2019, 100 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```

## Notes on Disparities Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of "Other race(s)" may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results. The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate. State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people. ::: ::: {.cell} ::: --- title: "South Dakota" ---
